skill.name: image-edit
skill.description: Edit images on RunComfy — this skill is a smart router that matches the user's intent to the right edit model in the RunComfy catalog. Picks Nano Banana Edit (batch up to 20, identity-preserving default), OpenAI GPT Image 2 Edit (multilingual in-image text rewrite, multi-ref composition, layout precision), Flux Kontext Pro (single-ref high-fidelity local edit), or Z-Image Turbo Inpaint (mask-driven precise region edit). Bundles each model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper edits without burning iterations on the wrong model. Calls `runcomfy run <vendor>/<model>/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "image edit", "edit image", "image-to-image", "i2i", "swap background", "remove object", "rewrite headline", or any explicit ask to edit a single or batch of images.
skill.summary: Intent-routed image editing on RunComfy that picks the best model (Nano Banana Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, Flux Kontext Pro, or Z-Image Inpaint) for each task.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 131 potential issues, but evaluation confirms most are false positives from Markdown documentation patterns: backticks around CLI command names, hardcoded documentation URLs to runcomfy.com, and `<absolute/path>` placeholders in example bash snippets. The SKILL.md is a pure documentation/routing layer for the runcomfy CLI; it contains no executable code. The skill explicitly documents its security model: token storage at `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with mode 0600, prompt content passed as a JSON string (not shell-expanded), outbound network restricted to `model-api.runcomfy.net` and `*.runcomfy.net` / `*.runcomfy.com`, and a 2 GiB cap on generated-file downloads. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or obfuscation patterns were found.

high_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern detected (false positive)
high_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged a 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at SKILL.md:4, SKILL.md:245, and SKILL.md:247. Manual review confirms these lines contain the frontmatter description field and the Limitations / Exit codes sections. No cryptographic code is present anywhere in the file. The 'weak crypto' pattern appears to be a generic regex overmatch on text content; dismissed as a false positive.

medium_findings.title: Skill invokes external runcomfy CLI commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to invoke `runcomfy run <vendor>/<model>/edit` shell commands with user-supplied image URLs and a prompt string. The CLI transmits the prompt as a JSON string (not via shell), so there is no shell-injection surface from prompt content. Network calls and file outputs are routed through the runcomfy CLI, not by this skill directly. This is a documented, intentional use of an external CLI.

medium_findings.title: User-supplied image URLs are fetched by the model server
medium_findings.description: The skill accepts HTTPS image and mask URLs from the user and passes them to the model API. As the skill's own Security & Privacy section notes, 'Treat external URLs as untrusted; image-based prompt injection is a known risk for any image-edit / video-edit model.' This is a documented third-party-content risk, not a vulnerability in the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded documentation URLs in skill content
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md contains 20 hardcoded HTTPS URLs. These are documentation references (runcomfy.com marketing, model catalog pages, GitHub repo, troubleshooting docs) and example image URLs in code snippets. None are credential exfiltration endpoints; all point to the documented RunComfy service.

low_findings.title: Hidden config file in user home directory
low_findings.description: The skill documents that `runcomfy login` writes the API token to `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with mode 0600 (owner-only read/write). This follows the XDG Base Directory convention and applies restrictive file permissions. The user can alternatively set `RUNCOMFY_TOKEN` env var to bypass the file entirely in CI.

content.user_title: Edit images on RunComfy with smart model routing
content.value_statement: Choosing the right AI model for each image edit wastes time and iterations. This skill reads your intent and routes to the best RunComfy model automatically, with documented prompting patterns for each route.
actual_capabilities: Routes user intent to one of four RunComfy edit models: Nano Banana Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, Flux Kontext Pro, or Z-Image Turbo Inpaint
Supports batch image editing of 1-20 images with locked aspect ratio and resolution
Rewrites in-image text in multiple languages including Japanese kana, Cyrillic, and Arabic
Composes multi-reference images with numbered source references for subject, scene, and palette
Performs mask-driven inpainting for precise object removal and region replacement
Invokes the runcomfy CLI with the correct model ID, JSON input body, and output directory
limitations: Picks one model per call; does not blend results across multiple routes in a single invocation
Each route inherits its underlying model's limits: Nano Banana 1-20 inputs, GPT Image 2 up to 10 refs, Flux Kontext single ref, Z-Image Inpaint requires a mask
Requires the runcomfy CLI installed globally and a valid runcomfy account or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var
Image and mask URLs must be publicly fetchable over HTTPS; local file paths are not supported

use_cases.title: E-commerce product photography cleanup
use_cases.description: Batch-process product images to remove watermarks, swap backgrounds, and standardize framing across an entire SKU catalog.

use_cases.title: Multilingual marketing asset localization
use_cases.description: Rewrite in-image headlines in Japanese, Cyrillic, or Arabic while keeping the brand mark, layout, and photographic content intact.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing localization leads and creative directors

use_cases.title: Visual asset retouching and cleanup
use_cases.description: Remove distracting overhead cables, watermarks, or background clutter from real-estate, travel, or event photography using mask-driven inpainting.
use_cases.target_user: Real-estate agents, travel photographers, and event videographers

prompt_templates.title: Single image background swap with identity preservation
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the background with a rainy neon cyberpunk street. Keep the subject identity, pose, clothing, and framing exactly as in the input.
prompt_templates.scenario: Default Nano Banana Edit for a single subject portrait

prompt_templates.title: Batch watermark removal with locked output format
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the watermark in the bottom-right corner. Lock aspect ratio to 1:1 and resolution to 1K across all inputs. Keep every other element unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: Nano Banana Edit for batch SKU gallery consistency

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual headline rewrite
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the photograph, layout, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the in-image headline. The new headline reads in bold Japanese kana, same position and font weight.
prompt_templates.scenario: GPT Image 2 Edit for non-Latin in-image text replacement

prompt_templates.title: Mask-driven object removal
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove overhead cables; preserve rooflines and sky gradient; thin clean sky. Strength 0.5, control_scale 0.8.
prompt_templates.scenario: Z-Image Turbo Inpaint for clean retouching of travel photos

output_examples.input: User asks: 'Take this portrait of me and put me in a Tokyo alley at night with neon signs. Keep my face and red jacket the same.'
output: Classified intent: background swap with identity preservation
Selected route: Nano Banana Edit (single image, general edit default)
Invocation: runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit with --input JSON containing the prompt and the user's image URL, --output-dir set to the workspace path
Tip emitted: 'Lock aspect_ratio to auto to match input framing across re-runs.'

output_examples.input: User provides 12 product photos and asks: 'Strip the watermark from the bottom-right of each. Keep the products identical.'
output: Classified intent: batch edit with consistent output format
Selected route: Nano Banana Edit with aspect_ratio locked to 1:1 and resolution to 1K
Invocation: runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit with --input JSON containing the prompt and all 12 image_urls, --output-dir set to the workspace path
Tip emitted: 'Iteration tip: if a single image drifts, isolate it and re-run on that one URL only.'

best_practices: Lead every prompt with a preservation clause: 'Keep [identity / pose / framing] unchanged' before stating the change.
Lock aspect_ratio and resolution when batching more than 3 images to prevent drift across the series.
Use mask-driven Z-Image Inpaint for precise cleanup of cables, watermarks, or backgrounds rather than language-only edits.
Quote in-image text exactly and name the script for non-Latin text to get cleaner typography in GPT Image 2 Edit.
Set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var in CI or containers instead of writing a token file.
anti_patterns: Do not chain multiple unrelated edits into a single pass; split compound edits into sequential shorter invocations to avoid drift.
Do not pass local file paths to image_urls; the model server requires publicly-fetchable HTTPS URLs.
Do not use Z-Image Inpaint without a mask; the route requires a grayscale mask URL and will fail without it.
Do not invoke a specific model by name and expect this router to override it; the skill respects explicit model requests and routes to the corresponding brand-specific skill instead.

faq.question: Which model does this skill pick by default?
faq.answer: Nano Banana Edit is the default for general edits and batch work because it supports both single images and batches up to 20, with strong identity preservation under preservation-first prompts.

faq.question: Do I need a runcomfy account to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. Run `npm i -g @runcomfy/cli` and then `runcomfy login` to authenticate, or set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable in CI and containers.

faq.question: Can the skill edit multiple images in one call?
faq.answer: Yes when routed to Nano Banana Edit (up to 20 inputs) or GPT Image 2 Edit (up to 10 refs). Flux Kontext Pro and Z-Image Inpaint are single-image routes.

faq.question: What languages are supported for in-image text rewrites?
faq.answer: GPT Image 2 Edit is the recommended route for multilingual text including Japanese kana, Cyrillic, and Arabic. Name the script explicitly in the prompt for best results.

faq.question: Where is my API token stored?
faq.answer: The runcomfy CLI writes the token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. You can set RUNCOMFY_TOKEN as an env var to avoid writing any file in CI or containers.

faq.question: Are my image URLs sent anywhere outside RunComfy?
faq.answer: The skill only invokes the runcomfy CLI, which submits requests to model-api.runcomfy.net and downloads outputs from *.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.com. There is no telemetry or callback to other endpoints.
